structured_payload_candidate.parameter_name: Humidity control challenge in humid climates
structured_payload_candidate.title: Humid Climate Warehouse Humidity Control
structured_payload_candidate.summary: In humid climates, space humidity can become unacceptably high when cooling equipment is off due to infiltration of moist air.

evidence_text:
WAREHOUSES AND DISTRIBUTION CENTERS
General Design Considerations
Warehouses can be defined as facilities that provide proper envi-
ronment for the purpose of storing goods and materials. They are
also used to store equipment and material inventory at industrialfacilities. At times, warehouses  may be open to the public. The
buildings are generally not air conditioned, but often have sufficient
heat and ventilation to provide a tolerable working environment. In
many cases, associated facilities occupied by office workers, such as
shipping, receiving, and inventor y control offices, are air condi-
tioned. Warehouses must be designed to accommodate the loads of
materials to be stored, associated  handling equipmen t, receiving and
shipping operations and associated trucking, and needs of operating
personnel.
judge_reason: Summary is about humidity control, but evidence text does not mention humidity, infiltration, or cooling equipment off states; evidence mismatch.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Fume Hood Type Application
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Variable Volume fume hoods are for moderate to highly hazardous processes with varying procedures.

evidence_text:
Licensed for single user. © 2019 ASHRAE, Inc.3.8 2019 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications (SI)
Cruise Terminals. Ventilation design must ensure that fumes
and odors from forklifts and cargo in work areas do not penetrate
occupied and administrative areas.
Bus Terminals. The primary concerns with enclosed bus loading
areas are health and safety probl ems, which must be handled by
proper ventilation (see Chapter 16). Although diesel  engine fumes
are generally not as noxious as ga soline fumes, bus terminals often
have many buses loading and unloading at the same time, and thetotal amount of fumes and odors may be disturbing.
In terms of health and safety, enclosed bus loading areas and
automobile parking garages pres ent the most serious problems.
Three major problems are encountered , the first and most serious of
which is emission of carbon monoxide (CO) by cars and oxides of
nitrogen (NO
x) by buses, which can caus e serious illness and pos-
sibly death.
judge_reason: Evidence is general text about bus terminals and parking garages, no mention of fume hood types; payload unsupported by evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.parameter_name: Mandatory safety feature for perchloric acid exhaust systems
structured_payload_candidate.title: Perchloric Acid Hood Requirement
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Perchloric acid exhaust systems must be equipped with an internal water washdown and drainage system due to explosion hazard.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Cultural Heritage Zoning
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Activities that pose a potential threat to collections environments should be kept away from collections zones, with exhaust from these areas located away and downwind from any fresh air intakes.

structured_payload_candidate.parameter_name: Alternatives for containment of preservation environments
structured_payload_candidate.title: Cultural Heritage Envelope Alternatives
structured_payload_candidate.summary: If envelope performance is questionable, consider alternatives like microenvironments, offsite storage, or box-in-box solutions.

structured_payload_candidate.parameter_name: Importance of latent capacity in cultural heritage applications
structured_payload_candidate.title: Cultural Heritage Latent Capacity
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Adequate latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard package unit designs, leading to unsafe relative humidity conditions.

structured_payload_candidate.parameter_name: Primary acoustic design method for HVAC systems
structured_payload_candidate.title: Acoustic Design Criteria
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The Noise Criteria (NC) method is the most commonly used for HVAC system noise design.
